mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW Z4 M engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S54 engine typically costs £5,500–£9,000 supply only. Used engines are available from £4,500–£7,500. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on your location and workshop. A replacement S54 engine costs on average £7,624, with reconditioned engines costing up to £9,926. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW Z4 M engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S54 is one of the greatest engines ever built — smooth, powerful, and with a glorious soundtrack. However, it has known weak points: rod bearing wear is the most notorious, with some owners reporting failures above 75,000 miles. The Z4 M's S54 is generally considered more reliable than the E46 M3's version, as BMW addressed many early VANOS issues. With proper maintenance, the S54 is a durable and rewarding engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW Z4 M?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are rod bearing wear (metallic knocking, engine failure risk), VANOS solenoid block contamination (rough idle, hard starts), oil leaks from the valve cover and oil filter housing, lifter tick, camshaft position sensor failure, and water pump failure. The S54 is a high-strung performance engine that demands meticulous maintenance.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW Z4 M engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £30,000 Z4 M, a £7,000 recon is 23% — worth it. Against a tired £12,000 example, the same recon approaches our 70% BMW threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Well-maintained Z4 Ms are appreciating rapidly — a proper restoration is often justified.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW Z4 M engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The Z4 M carries the engine code S54B32. It's a 3.2-litre straight-six naturally aspirated engine used exclusively in the Z4 M (E85/E86) from 2006 to 2008.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the rod bearings, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — rod bearing replacement is a well-understood preventative repair costing £1,500–£3,500. Many owners replace rod bearings proactively as preventative maintenance, as the cost of bearing failure is catastrophic — a failed bearing can destroy the crankshaft and require a full engine replacement (£5,500–£12,000). If you hear knocking from the bottom end, do not drive the car. Regular oil analysis can help monitor bearing health.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW Z4 M Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: No longer available from BMW — the S54 is out of production

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the S54B32 3.2-litre straight-six engine. The S54 commands a significant premium over standard Z4 engines due to its M-car status and limited production. A replacement S54 engine costs on average £7,624, with reconditioned engines costing up to £9,926. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Z4 M engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £80–£120/hr (independent) or £140–£200/hr (London/SE dealer). The S54's complexity and the Z4's tight engine bay make this a specialist job.

cta.label: → Compare Z4 M engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Rod Bearing Wear (S54)
problems.symptoms: Metallic knocking noise from the bottom end, especially under load, low oil pressure warning, engine failure at high RPM, oil debris visible when draining.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£3,500 (rod bearing replacement — preventative)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£12,000 (if bearing fails and destroys the crankshaft)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear knocking, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: Rod bearing wear is the S54's most notorious issue. The original bearings have tight clearances and can wear prematurely, especially on cars that are tracked or driven hard. Many owners proactively replace rod bearings as preventative maintenance. If you hear knocking from the bottom end, do not drive the car — catastrophic engine failure is imminent. Regular oil analysis can help monitor bearing health.

failureLink.label: Read the full rod bearing failure guide

problems.title: VANOS Solenoid Block Contamination
problems.symptoms: Rough idling, hesitation on acceleration, hard starting, loss of low-end power, engine warning light, VANOS rattle.
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (VANOS solenoid block cleaning or replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: The S54's VANOS solenoid block can become restricted with debris over time, causing rough idling, hard starts, and VANOS noise. The Z4 M's S54 is generally considered more reliable than the E46 M3's version, as BMW addressed many early VANOS issues. However, VANOS maintenance remains important. Cleaning or replacing the solenoid block is a well-understood repair.

failureLink.label: Read the full VANOS failure guide

problems.title: Oil Leaks & Cooling System Issues
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell from the engine bay, low oil level warning, visible oil residue around the valve cover or oil filter housing area; overheating, coolant loss.
problems.repairCost: £300–£700 (gasket replacement) / £400–£900 (water pump replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: Oil leaks from the valve cover and oil filter housing are common on ageing S54 engines. The electric water pump is also a known failure point — if it fails, the car will overheat at low engine speed, such as sitting at a stoplight. If you notice oil spots or coolant loss, have the engine inspected promptly.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Z4 M — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — far cheaper than a full engine replacement
Well-understood fix; cheaper than engine replacement
Standard maintenance on ageing M engines
Simple repair; part of routine maintenance

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Z4 M? Check These First
buyIf: * VANOS solenoid block cleaned or replaced within the last 30,000 miles
* Full service history with evidence of regular oil changes using BMW 10W-60 M-specific oil
* No cold-start rattle, hesitation, or knocking
avoidIf: * Knocking from the bottom end (rod bearing failure warning)
* No proof of rod bearing maintenance — this is a when, not an if
* Poor service history — the S54 is unforgiving of neglect
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis
* Evidence of overheating or cooling system issues

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Z4 M is a true future classic — the last naturally aspirated M engine in a lightweight roadster chassis. The S54 is one of the greatest engines ever built, but it has known weak points: rod bearings, VANOS, and oil leaks. The Z4 M's S54 is generally more reliable than the E46 M3's version, as BMW addressed many early issues. However, rod bearings remain the single biggest concern — many owners replace them proactively as preventative maintenance. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained Z4 M is a £20,000–£40,000 future classic; a neglected one is a £10,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare Z4 M engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW Z4 M (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for knocking, rattling, or hesitation (rod bearings or VANOS warning)
* Service History — including the S54 era (2006–2008) and confirmed oil-change intervals using BMW 10W-60 M-specific oil
* Rod Bearing Check — Any history of rod bearing replacement? (preventative replacement is a major plus)
* VANOS Check — Any rough idling, hesitation, or hard starting? (VANOS solenoid warning)
* Oil Leak Check — Any evidence of oil leaks around the valve cover or oil filter housing?
* Coolant Check — Any history of coolant loss or water pump replacement?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved VANOS, cam sensor, or cooling system issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
